turns.text: Hello Agnes, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you. I... I appreciate you asking. Honestly, it’s a bit strange to talk about it out loud. Most days, I just keep it to myself, watching the fog move over the trees here in Grafton and trying to figure out how to be a person who is suddenly responsible for another person. 

I’m only nineteen. I look in the mirror and I still feel like a kid, but then the nausea hits—that metallic taste that never really leaves—and I know it’s real. My doctor says I’m “low-risk,” which I guess is good, but it doesn't make me feel any less scared. The clinic is such a long drive from here, past all these empty fields where there’s nobody to call if things go wrong. 

When I’m there, in the exam room, I feel like I have to play this part. I nod at the brochures and try to look like I know what I’m doing, but I’m terrified to ask questions. I don’t want them to look at me—at my age, without a ring on my finger—and think I’m just some girl who doesn't know better. I just want to know if the cramps are normal or if I’m losing it. Does that make sense? It feels like I’m holding my breath, waiting for someone to tell me I’m actually going to be okay at this.

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

I think I expected it to be… I don't know, a bit more personal? Like in the movies, where there’s a doctor who sits you down, holds your hand, and explains exactly what’s happening inside you. I thought they’d be mentors, I guess. I imagined walking into an office and having someone see me—not just the chart, not just my age—and really guiding me through the transition of becoming a mother.

But the reality? It’s cold. It’s clinical. Everything is fast. I go in, I pee in a cup, I get weighed, and they give me these glossy pamphlets that seem written for someone else, someone older with a house and a husband. They check the boxes on their screens, tell me my vitals are fine, and send me on my way. It feels less like care and more like a transaction. 

I’m left with these questions that feel too "stupid" or too emotional for a ten-minute window. Sometimes, I’m just sitting there in that paper gown, looking at the nurse’s back, and I want to ask her, "Do you think I can actually do this?" But I never do. I just take the papers, put on my coat, and drive back to the silence of the house. It’s lonely, feeling like I’m just a data point in their system.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — when you first engaged with a healthcare provider about trying to conceive?

The biggest surprise, I think, was just how invisible I felt. I suppose I naively expected that because I’m technically "low-risk," there would be some sort of excitement or at least a bit of warmth. Like, "Congratulations, here’s what to look forward to." Instead, it was like I had walked into a factory. 

There wasn’t any conversation about *me*—about how it feels to be nineteen and terrified, or how I’m trying to juggle finding full-time work while my body feels like it’s betraying me with this constant, dragging exhaustion. They were focused entirely on the biology, the dates, and the tests. 

But maybe the most negative surprise was the judgment I feel—or maybe I’m projecting it—when they realize I’m on my own. I saw the nurse’s eyes shift toward my file when she saw there was no partner listed. That silence in the room suddenly felt heavy, like she was silently assessing my capability or my life choices rather than my health. It made me shut down. I had all these questions about morning sickness and what I should be eating, but once I felt that flicker of judgment, I just retreated. I realized then that I wasn't there to be supported; I was there to be managed. It’s a very lonely realization when you’re already scared.
Before trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And how did your emotional state actually compare to those expectations once the process began?

I think I always thought "ready" meant having this overwhelming, golden feeling of excitement. Like, if I was really ready, I’d be picking out paint colors for a nursery or feeling this deep, calm confidence that I knew exactly how to be a mom. I expected to feel like an adult, I guess. I thought that by the time I was actually pregnant, the fear would just vanish, replaced by that maternal glow you see in magazines.

But the reality is… I feel like I’m playing house in a life I’m not quite grown into yet. Instead of calm, I feel this jagged, constant anxiety. Every time my body changes—the nausea, the way my clothes feel tighter, the weird, sharp aches—I don’t feel "empowered." I feel like a stranger in my own skin. 

It’s been a shock. I didn’t realize that the emotional toll would be so tied to the isolation. I thought I’d have a team around me, but most nights, it’s just me and my laptop, looking up symptoms because I’m too embarrassed to ask the doctor. I’m not a bad person, but I feel like I’m failing because I’m so often just… terrified. I expected to feel like a mother-in-waiting. Mostly, I just feel like a teenager who made a big change and now has to figure out the rest of the world while the fog keeps rolling in.
Before conception, what did you expect to find when you looked for information online or through apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

Before I started this, I thought the internet would be this clear, helpful map. I figured I’d find forums full of women who were just like me—sharing the same fears, maybe giving a little advice on how to handle the nerves. I thought I’d find straightforward answers that would make me feel like I was checking off a list of "to-dos" until everything was handled.

But when I actually started searching, it was like hitting a wall of, I don't know, *perfection*. Every app and website seems aimed at a "perfect" pregnancy—a woman who is stable, married, and has a whole support system cheering her on. It’s all about "baby showers" and "nursery themes" and "how to involve your partner." It makes me feel like I’m doing this wrong because my reality doesn't look like their stock photos. 

Then there’s the medical stuff. If I search for a symptom—like the sharp, scary cramps I get—the forums immediately jump to the worst-case scenarios, or they use all these cold, confusing terms that just spike my heart rate. I’m looking for someone to say, "Hey, it’s okay to be young and scared," but instead, I just find scary statistics or happy, shiny advice that feels completely out of touch with my life in Grafton. It’s not what I need at all. I need a human, not a search result.
What was the biggest gap between what digital sources told you and what your healthcare provider told you?

The biggest gap is definitely the tone. When I go to the doctor, they treat everything like it’s a standard, boring procedure. They’re so focused on the "low-risk" box that they don’t acknowledge the human side—the fear, the isolation, the fact that my life is fundamentally changing. They act like if my blood pressure and weight are okay, then I’m perfectly fine.

The internet, on the other hand, is the complete opposite extreme. It’s either hyper-sanitized, "perfect-life" stuff that makes me feel like I’m failing because I’m stressed and broke, or it’s the worst-case scenario. If I look up a minor ache online, the forums are full of people shouting about miscarriages and emergencies, which makes me want to panic.

My doctor says, "Everything is normal, don't worry," but the internet says, "This could be a sign of everything going wrong." Neither one of them actually talks to me like I’m a person. The doctor talks to a patient file, and the internet talks to a terrified stranger. I feel like there’s this huge, empty space in the middle where a real conversation about being scared and nineteen is supposed to happen, but nobody is standing there to fill it. It’s like they’re speaking two different languages, and I’m just trying to survive in the silence between them.
If you had a pre-existing health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? And what actually happened when you sought that help?

I don't have a chronic, named condition like diabetes or something you’d find on a chart, but I’ve struggled with anxiety for a long time. Before I got pregnant, I guess I naively hoped that telling my provider about that would trigger some kind of... I don't know, a check-in? A conversation about how to keep my head above water while my hormones were doing all this other stuff? I expected them to be proactive, maybe suggest someone to talk to or just acknowledge that the mental side of this is as big a deal as the physical side.

What actually happened was pretty discouraging. When I mentioned feeling really overwhelmed, the doctor just gave me a dismissive wave and said, "That’s just the hormones, dear, it’s completely normal." Then she handed me a pamphlet about nutrition and moved on to checking my blood pressure. 

It made me feel like my feelings were just a nuisance, a side effect to be ignored rather than something I needed help managing. They act like if my physical numbers are fine, my mental state doesn't matter. But out here, when I’m staring at those trees and the fog, my thoughts are the hardest part to manage. Being dismissed like that made me feel even more isolated, like I’m supposed to just "suck it up" and be a perfect, calm little vessel.
Did you feel you had to advocate strongly for yourself, or did the system proactively reach out to support you?

The system has never reached out to me, not once. If I don't call them, if I don't show up at the clinic at the exact time they told me to, I’m basically invisible. It’s definitely not proactive. 

I’ve learned pretty quickly that if I don’t advocate for myself, I’m just going to be treated like a ghost in the exam room. But the problem is, *how* do you advocate for yourself when you’re nineteen and you feel like the "odd one out"? I’m so afraid that if I push back or ask too many questions, they’ll think I’m being difficult, or worse, they’ll think I’m incapable of being a mother because I’m "too stressed." 

So, I’m stuck in this awful middle ground. I’m not getting the support I need because I’m scared to speak up, and I’m scared to speak up because I feel like the system is looking for a reason to judge me. I’m basically just nodding along, waiting for the appointments to end. It’s not advocacy; it’s survival. I’m just trying to make it to the next milestone without being seen as a problem. It’s exhausting to have to be your own expert when you’re the one who is supposed to be receiving the care.
Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

I think that’s the part that hurts the most to talk about. Even before I knew I was pregnant, I had this picture in my head—I suppose everyone does—of sharing the discovery, of sitting together in the evenings and talking about the future, of having someone to drive me to those long, cold appointments in Grafton so I wasn't just staring at the road alone. I wanted a partner who would be curious with me, someone who would read those brochures and ask the questions I was too nervous to voice.

The reality is that there is no partner. It’s just me. And honestly, it’s a silence that screams. When I’m at the clinic, I have to fill out those forms that ask for a partner's name or contact info, and I just leave the lines blank. It feels like a neon sign pointing at me, saying, "She’s alone, she’s young, she’s doing this the hard way."

I imagined a teammate, but instead, I’m the sole manager of this entire, frightening process. It makes the weight of it feel twice as heavy. I don't have anyone to turn to when I wake up in the middle of the night terrified; I just have to be my own comfort, and I’m really not sure I’m strong enough to be both the parent and the support system all at once.
Were there moments where you felt the burden of preparation fell disproportionately on you? What drove that?

Every single moment feels like that, honestly. It’s not just the doctor’s appointments; it’s every single day. I’m the one who has to scour the internet at midnight to figure out why I feel like my body is stretching apart. I’m the one who has to plan how to keep my job while I’m constantly nauseated, and I’m the one who has to stare at the grocery store shelves and try to guess what I can afford to eat that will actually keep the baby healthy.

I think the reason it falls so entirely on me is that I am, quite literally, alone. There’s no one to share the mental load with. When you’re nineteen and isolated in a place like Grafton, the "preparation" isn't just about vitamins and doctor visits—it’s about trying to grow up overnight. I feel like I’m the only one in the room when it comes to every decision, every fear, and every "what if."

The system doesn't help with that burden either. They don't look at my life and say, "How are you managing this alone?" They just see a patient ID number. Because I don't have a partner to handle the logistical or emotional weight, it all lands on my shoulders. I’m just trying to learn to carry it without buckling, but some days the weight of it—the sheer loneliness of it—is almost more than I can take.
If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

I haven't had a loss—this is my first time being pregnant, and that’s part of why I’m so anxious. I’ve read all the forums and seen all the stories online about how things can just stop, and because I’m so isolated here in the woods, I don’t have anyone to tell me if what I’m feeling is normal or a red flag. 

I think that's why the silence from the healthcare system is so loud for me. I expected that maybe, even if I’m "low-risk," they would offer a little bit of extra reassurance because they know how much I don't know. I wanted someone to say, "If you feel *this*, call us," or "Here is someone you can talk to if you're feeling scared about a potential loss." 

But they don't say anything. They act like pregnancy is this perfect, guaranteed line from A to B as long as I show up for my check-ups. It makes me feel like if something *did* go wrong, I’d be completely on my own to figure out what was happening. It’s that constant, low-level hum of dread in the back of my mind—that if I’m not smart enough or careful enough, I could lose this, and no one would even know I was struggling until it was too late. I feel like I'm walking a tightrope without a net.
Was there a point where you felt your previous loss was not being taken seriously enough in the context of your new pregnancy attempt?

As I said, this is my first pregnancy, so I don't have the history of a loss to point to. But, honestly? It feels like the *fear* of that loss—the fear that everything could just stop—is something the clinic treats like it’s imaginary, or just a part of being a "nervous" teenager.

Even without a previous loss, that shadow is always right behind me. When I have those sharp, stabbing cramps and I’m shaking in my kitchen, I don't know if that’s just my body growing or if it’s the beginning of a tragedy. When I try to bring those worries up, the doctors look at me like I’m asking for a security blanket I don’t need. It’s like, because I’m "low-risk" on paper, they don't think I have any reason to be scared. 

But I am. Every single hour. I feel like my body is this fragile, dangerous place, and because I’ve never done this before, I don't know the difference between "normal discomfort" and something genuinely wrong. It feels like my internal alarm bells aren't being taken seriously at all. To them, it’s just another day in the clinic. To me, it’s the most important, terrifying thing that has ever happened in my life, and I feel like I’m shouting into a vacuum.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

I guess I pictured a sanctuary. I thought the clinic would feel like a place where they’d sit me down and truly *welcome* me to this new chapter. I imagined a quiet, soft-lit room where the doctor would explain the science of what was growing inside me, but also—and this is what I really wanted—they’d ask me how I was feeling about it all. I thought they’d be kind, reassuring people who’d see my age and, instead of judging, offer a little extra kindness. I wanted to feel like I was starting a journey with a team behind me.

What I got instead was a sterile, fluorescent-lit room that felt like a DMV waiting area. It was all hard plastic chairs and cold, intimidating equipment. The people were pleasant enough, I suppose, but they were robotic. It was just: "Step on the scale. Roll up your sleeve. Pee in this cup. Here’s a stack of papers."

They didn't talk to me; they talked at me, reciting things I’d already read online until my eyes glazed over. There was no warmth, no acknowledgement that I was scared, and certainly no one asking if I had support at home. It wasn’t a sanctuary. It was a conveyor belt. I walked out of that first appointment feeling smaller and more alone than when I walked in. It wasn't information I received; it was just a set of instructions for a job I didn't know how to do.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise was definitely how quickly they could strip the "person" out of the patient. I walked in there, my heart pounding, honestly hoping to find a mentor or someone who’d say, "You’re doing a great job, being here is the first step," but instead, I was a series of vitals and check-boxes. 

I think the biggest negative surprise was the sheer lack of curiosity about my life. I’m nineteen, I’m working, and I’m navigating all of this in a house that feels way too big and quiet in the mornings. I expected the doctor to ask, "How are you managing?" or "Do you have anyone to talk to?" but they didn't ask a single question about my world outside those four exam room walls. 

It made me realize that to them, my pregnancy is just a low-risk clinical event. They don’t see the girl behind the chart. I left feeling like a piece of equipment that needed a routine maintenance check, not a person going through the most terrifying, monumental change of her life. It was just so... hollow. That was the real shock: how little the medical system actually cares about the person *in* the body, as long as the body is doing what the textbook says it should do.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I really expected a conversation. You see it in movies, right? Someone sits you down, pulls up a monitor, points at the screen, and says, "Look, here’s the heartbeat, here’s where they’re growing, and here’s what this means for you." I expected a sense of shared discovery—that someone would actually *explain* the images to me in plain English so I could finally breathe a sigh of relief. I thought I’d leave that room feeling like I understood what was happening inside my own body.

The reality was… dismissive. The person doing the scan was mostly focused on getting the right angles for their measurements. They didn’t really talk to me; they just muttered numbers to themselves or made notes. When they were done, they just said, "Everything looks normal," and sent me back to the waiting area to wait for the doctor to sign off on it. 

When the results actually came back, it was just a message on an app or a quick, rushed phone call from a nurse who seemed like she had three other things to do. There was no "you're doing great" or "this is what we're looking for next." It felt like they were withholding the "why" and just giving me the "yes." It left me more confused than before, still staring at that grainy photo, wondering if I’m actually seeing a baby or just shadows, because nobody took the time to bridge that gap between the technology and my own heart.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I really, truly expected to have one person—maybe a midwife or even just a dedicated doctor—who would be *my* person. I thought that by the second or third visit, they’d walk into the room, remember my name without looking at the chart, and ask, "How have you been, Agnes? How are those cramps? How is that job hunt going?" I wanted that continuity because I figured that if they knew me, they’d know when I was scared or when I was just being a first-time mom. I thought it would be like having an older, wiser person on my side.

The reality is that I never see the same person twice. It’s a revolving door of nurses and residents. Every single time I go into that clinic, I have to re-explain who I am, why I’m there, and that I’m nineteen and on my own. I have to summarize my fears all over again to someone who is clearly just passing through their rotation. 

It feels like I’m starting from zero every single time. There is absolutely no continuity of care. I’m just a face in a paper gown that they’ll forget the second I walk out the door. It makes me feel incredibly small and entirely alone in this. How can I feel like my care is being managed when the person I’m talking to doesn't even know what I said at the last appointment?
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

Practically, it’s a nightmare because I have to repeat myself over and over. I walk in, and the new person asks, "Any concerns?" and I have to remember if I brought up the nausea to the last person, or if they dismissed it already, or if I should even bother saying it again. It feels like I’m wasting their time. 

Emotionally? It makes me feel invisible. Every time I have to re-explain that I'm here by myself, I see that same flicker of judgment or just plain indifference in their eyes—the look that says, "Okay, another young, single, stressed-out girl." There’s no rapport. I can’t build any trust with a revolving door of people who are looking at their watches. 

I’ve stopped asking the questions that actually matter to me—the ones about how I'm going to handle being a mother at nineteen, or the fear that I’m just not *ready*—because I know they aren't invested in my life. They’re just checking boxes. It’s impossible to feel safe when you have to re-introduce your deepest anxieties to a stranger every two weeks. You end up just giving them the "I’m fine" answer because it’s easier than trying to form a human connection with someone who is just counting the minutes until they can leave the room. It’s like I’m fighting to be recognized as a person in my own pregnancy.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I think I expected it to be a conversation. I imagined they’d say, "You might notice this happening around now, it’s normal, here’s why." I thought they’d explain *what* they were feeling for, and *why* they were measuring me, so I’d feel like a partner in the process instead of just a body being poked and prodded. I wanted them to treat my body like it belonged to me—like it was something we were both looking after.

But the reality? It’s completely clinical and cold. They come into the room, tell me to hop on the table, and then they start pressing down on my stomach or checking me without saying a word. They treat my body like it’s just a piece of machinery they’re inspecting during a shift. They don't look me in the eye when they’re examining me; they’re just looking at their charts or their notes.

It feels… violating, almost. Like I’m just an object they’ve been assigned to check for an hour. There’s no warmth, no gentleness. It just reinforces that feeling that I’m just a kid, and they’re the ones in charge of this thing growing inside me. I want to say, "Hey, that hurts, can you explain what you’re doing?" but I feel so small in that position. It’s supposed to be *my* pregnancy, but I feel like I’m barely a part of the conversation.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

It was during one of my first internal exams—I wasn't prepared for how quick and clinical it would be. I hadn't been told exactly what to expect, and suddenly there were these cold instruments, and the person just started working without a single word of warning. I was lying there, feeling completely exposed and small, staring at a stain on the ceiling tile, while they were just talking to a student or a nurse about a different patient's chart.

I wanted to ask them to slow down, to explain what they were looking for, but I felt so intimidated. I just kept thinking, "If I speak up, they're going to think I'm 'that' patient—the difficult, young, single one who doesn't know how to follow directions." So I just clamped my jaw shut and tried to disappear into the table. 

That whole interaction left me feeling like a specimen, not a woman. It’s hard to feel like you’re doing something as big and beautiful as growing a baby when you’re treated like you’re just a body part that needs to be checked and cleared. It wasn't just physical discomfort; it was the feeling of being completely ignored while my body was being handled. I left that room feeling colder than when I went in, and I didn't want to go back for the next one.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I haven't had a diagnosis like that—and honestly, that’s one of the things I spend half my nights awake worrying about. I think because I’m already feeling so overwhelmed by the "normal" parts of being nineteen and on my own, the idea of having to manage a high-risk condition feels like a cliff I’m about to fall off.

If I did have something like that, I think I’d expect the system to be *more* involved, not less. I’d expect a plan, maybe a nurse navigator or someone who actually checks in on me between those long drives to the clinic. I’d expect them to understand that it’s not just about "checking my sugar" or "monitoring blood pressure," but about managing the terror of having a complication when I’m already feeling so fragile.

But seeing how the system has treated me so far—the revolving door of providers, the lack of warmth, the way they barely have time to look me in the eye—it makes me terrified that if I *did* get a high-risk diagnosis, I’d just be handed more brochures and left to figure it out alone. It’s like they assume I have a stable, supportive life that can absorb the extra work of a medical complication. If I’m already invisible, how much more invisible would I be if I actually needed real, constant care? It’s a scary thought.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected that if I was genuinely worried—like if I felt that sharp, frightening cramping that happens at night—I’d have someone I could call, a real person who would listen and tell me, "Agnes, you’re safe, here’s what’s happening," or "Come in, we want to see you." I thought there would be a safety net. After all, I’m "low-risk," but I’m still human, and I didn't think a system that prides itself on being professional would just leave a pregnant woman to sit in the dark and panic.

But the reality is I feel like a total burden to them. When I call the clinic, I get a generic automated menu, then a receptionist who acts like I’m asking for a favor just by needing a question answered. If I finally get a nurse on the line, they always ask, "Is it an emergency?" in this tone that makes it clear they hope I say "no." They make me feel like I’m being dramatic, or "hysterical," as if my fear is a personal failing.

They’re never proactive. They never check in. It’s always me, waiting on hold, feeling small, trying to decide if my fear is "enough" to justify a visit. I’ve learned that "prompt" doesn't exist. I’m just one more number in a crowded system, and if I’m not bleeding or in active labor, I’m clearly not a priority. It’s isolating. It makes me feel like I have to prove I’m in pain before anyone will give me the time of day.
Were there times you delayed seeking help because of how difficult it was to access care, or because you worried about being judged for contacting services?

Yes, almost every time. There was one night recently where the cramping was sharp—sharper than the usual stretching pains—and I spent hours lying in bed in the dark, staring at the ceiling, trying to convince myself I was just being a "paranoid nineteen-year-old." I had my phone in my hand, my thumb hovering over the clinic’s after-hours line, but I just couldn't make the call. 

I kept playing it out in my head: the nurse on the other end, bored and tired, sighing when I say my name. I could almost hear her voice, polite but clearly annoyed, telling me to "just take some Tylenol and wait for your appointment next week." The thought of that interaction, of being made to feel like a hysterical girl wasting their time, was honestly more painful than the cramping itself. 

So I didn't call. I just lay there, shivering, wondering if I was doing something dangerous by *not* calling, but paralyzed by the fear of being judged if I *did*. It’s a terrible place to be—stuck between the anxiety of something going wrong with the baby and the anxiety of being treated like a nuisance by the people who are supposed to be helping me. You shouldn't have to be brave to ask for medical care, but out here, in the quiet of this house, you absolutely do.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I think I expected—maybe hoped is the better word—that once people knew, there would be a sort of village. You hear about women being brought casseroles, or having someone to talk to about the clothes and the gear, or just someone to sit with when the fear gets too big. I expected my family to be part of that circle.

But the reality has been mostly silence, or worse, quiet, pointed glances. My family, they’re here, but they’re… distant. There’s no excitement. It’s like they’re waiting for me to fail, or maybe they’re just so uncomfortable with the situation that they’ve decided the easiest way to handle it is to pretend it isn't happening. My friends? They’re all finishing up their first year of college or planning road trips. They don't know what to say to a pregnant nineteen-year-old, so they just stop calling. 

I’m left navigating this alone in a house that feels way too big. Nobody asks how I’m doing, and if I try to say I’m scared, the subject gets changed. It’s like being pregnant has put me behind this invisible glass wall. I can see the world, but I’m not really a part of it anymore. It’s not just the medical side that’s lonely; it’s the lack of anyone who wants to hold my hand while I walk through this. I’m doing it, but I’m doing it entirely by myself.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I think I expected—or maybe just fantasized about—the kind of "it takes a village" support you read about. I pictured my aunt or a friend stopping by to drop off old baby clothes or just sitting in the kitchen with a cup of tea, asking how I was handling the nausea. I really expected that if I reached out, the people in my life would rally because, well, I'm nineteen and I'm doing this alone.

That didn't materialize. At all. Instead, there’s this awkward, heavy silence whenever I try to talk about the baby. It’s like they don’t know how to look at me without seeing a mistake. When I bring up the baby, they’ll offer a polite, tight-lipped smile and then change the subject to something safe, like the weather or the drive into town. It’s incredibly lonely.

What I *did* get, though, was a lot of unsolicited, sharp advice from people who don't know the first thing about my life. I have neighbors who make little comments about how I’m "so young" or how "everything is going to change." It feels less like support and more like judgment disguised as concern. Nobody has offered a hand, but plenty of people have offered a critique. It’s made me pull back completely. I’ve stopped trying to share the hard parts with anyone because I know the response won’t be "How can I help?" It’ll just be another look that says I’m in over my head.

It’s funny, the way things turn out. I used to think the hardest part of being pregnant would be the physical stuff—the fatigue, the nausea, the way my body feels like it's being reshaped by someone else's blueprints. But that’s the easy part. The real work is managing this constant, hum-like frequency of feeling "less than." 

When I look at the brochures from the clinic, with their pictures of happy, glowing couples in well-lit kitchens, I feel like I’m looking at a life that belongs to a different species. My life is foggy mornings in a quiet house, a job I’m struggling to keep, and the weight of every decision landing squarely on my shoulders because there’s no one to share the load. 

I’m starting to realize that the healthcare system, the community, the family—they all have this script they expect a mother to follow. And because I’m nineteen and alone, I’m clearly not reading from their version. I’m just trying to be a person who gets to have a healthy baby, but the system treats me like I’m a complication itself. 

I just wish someone would look at me—not at the chart, not at the missing partner, not at my age—and say, "You're a person, and you're doing okay." Just that. Just once. It’s a very quiet, cold world when you’re waiting for validation that never comes.
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

Every time I step into that clinic, it feels like my own intuition is treated as an annoyance. I remember one appointment, I tried to mention how exhausted I was—not just the "first trimester tired," but a deep, bone-crushing fatigue that made it hard to work. I wanted to talk about pacing myself, or maybe just having someone acknowledge that working full-time while my body is doing all this work is a lot.

The doctor didn’t even look up from his screen. He just said, "Well, you're young and healthy, so you should be able to handle it. It's just a normal part of pregnancy."

It felt like my reality was being erased by someone who doesn't live in my body. It wasn't just his opinion overriding mine; it was him telling me that what I was feeling wasn't real, or that it didn't matter. It silenced me. Why bother speaking up about how I’m struggling when the "expert" just tells me my struggles are "normal"?

It’s like that with my family, too. They’ll make comments about how I should be eating, or what I should be doing, as if they’re suddenly the authorities on my life now that there’s a baby involved. Nobody asks what *I* think or what *I* need. They just project their own ideas of what a "proper" pregnancy looks like onto me, and I’m expected to just be quiet and conform to their version of "doing it right." It’s exhausting to feel like I’m losing the right to my own experiences.

I think the worst part is the silence that follows. Like when I try to speak up, and they just nod and look back at their charts—that silence feels like an eraser. It’s like, for a second, I exist, and then they decide what I’m saying isn't relevant to the "clinical picture," so I’m wiped off the board again.

Sometimes I think about the life I had before this. It wasn’t perfect, but at least I felt like the person steering the ship. Now, I feel like a passenger in a car where someone else is driving, and they’re not even listening to me when I say the road ahead looks dangerous. 

I’m so tired of being "the nineteen-year-old girl." I want to be Agnes. I want someone to ask me what *my* fears are, not just what the checklist says. I want to tell someone that when I sit in my kitchen here in Grafton, watching the fog, I’m not just a set of vitals—I’m a person who is doing the hardest, most terrifying thing I’ve ever had to do, and I’m doing it with nobody in the passenger seat. 

If I could change one thing, it would just be for someone to listen. Not to correct me, not to judge me, not to tell me how "normal" it all is, but to just *hear* me. Because out here, in the quiet, my own voice is starting to sound like a stranger's. I’m scared that if I keep being treated like a statistic, I’m going to start believing I am one. And I’m more than that. I think I am. I hope I am.
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

It feels strange to even talk about "preferences" when there’s no partner in the room. But I suppose there's a different kind of pressure I feel—the pressure to align with the *phantom* of a partner. Everything about the brochures, the clinic, even the way people talk to me at the grocery store, assumes there’s a "we" making these decisions. 

I’m constantly pressured by that weight—the weight of what *should* be. Every time I walk into that clinic, they ask about my "support system" or "who I’m consulting with." When I have to say, "It’s just me," I can feel the judgment hanging in the air. It’s like they expect me to have some invisible partner whose opinion I should be weighing against my own. 

Even when I’m deciding on things like prenatal vitamins, or whether I can keep working full-time, I feel this internal pressure to prove I can "do it all" like a woman with a partner would. I’m pushing myself to show everyone that I don't need anyone else—that I’m strong enough, capable enough, and organized enough to do this alone. It’s like I’m in a constant contest with an imaginary standard. I’m not just making decisions for me and my baby; I’m making decisions to defend my right to be a mother on my own. It’s exhausting, trying to be two people at once.

You know, the thing I keep coming back to is how much I’ve had to perform. I walk into that clinic and I feel like I’m auditioning for the role of "Competent Patient." I wear the right clothes, I nod when they talk, I make sure my forms are filled out neatly so they can’t find any reason to think I’m "that" girl—the one who’s out of her depth. 

It’s like I’m building a wall out of politeness and "everything is fine" answers, hoping that if I make the wall high enough, nobody will notice how much I’m actually shaking. Because if they notice, then the judgment comes out. I can see it in their eyes—that subtle shift, the way they tighten their lips or shorten their sentences. They aren't looking at me, Agnes. They’re looking at a set of circumstances they think they understand, and they’ve already decided how the story should end.

I’m so tired of being reduced to a "case." I’m not a "low-risk case" who happens to be nineteen. I’m a person who is trying to build a future, sitting in this quiet house, trying to figure out how to love this little thing growing inside me when I don’t even have anyone to tell me I’m doing it right. 

I just wish the system would see that being "low-risk" physically doesn’t mean I’m "low-need" emotionally. I need someone to say, "It’s okay to be scared, and it’s okay to be alone, and you’re going to be a good mother." Is that really asking for so much? I don't want another glossy brochure. I want to be heard. I want to be seen. I just want to feel like I’m not doing this entirely in the dark.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

Oh, all the time. It’s like the second I showed up with a "pregnant" label, I lost the right to own my own body. It’s not just the doctors; it’s everyone. Someone will look at me—at my face or my stomach—and just say, "Oh, you’re so small, you don't even look like you’re carrying, make sure you’re eating enough," or they’ll make a comment about how "young" I look and how I need to "bulk up" for the baby’s sake.

They don’t see that I’m already working full-time and barely have the energy to stand, let alone cook some fancy, perfect meal. When they say those things, it’s like they’re accusing me of failing the baby before it’s even born. It hits me right in the gut. Every comment is like a little needle, pinning me down as "not enough."

It makes me want to shrink. I already feel so exposed, and their comments just make it feel like my body is public property. When a nurse or a stranger makes a comment about my size, I feel like they’re judging my capability as a mother based on my waistline. It makes me want to stop going out, to stay in this house until it’s all over, because at least here, in the quiet, nobody is telling me I’m not doing a good enough job of being a vessel. I’m just trying to survive the day, and they’re acting like I’m a science project they get to critique. It’s exhausting.

I think what’s hardest is that the world acts like my body belongs to the *idea* of a mother, rather than to me, Agnes. When someone at the grocery store or a nurse at the clinic makes a passing comment about how I look—"Oh, you’re so tiny, make sure you’re eating," or "You’re barely showing, are you taking your vitamins?"—it feels like a strike. They aren't asking how *I* feel; they’re telling me how I’m failing.

It’s like they see nineteen years of age and no partner and decide that I’m automatically irresponsible or uneducated. So, every physical comment is loaded with this unspoken, judgmental baggage: *She’s young, she’s single, she probably doesn’t know how to feed herself, let alone a baby.*

I spend my nights in this house in Grafton, scrolling through those forums, and I see the same thing there—people judging other women for how they carry, for what they weigh, for how much they eat. It makes me feel like I’m in a cage I didn’t build. I want to tell them that I’m trying, that I’m exhausted, and that the "smallness" they’re pointing out isn't a lack of effort—it’s just the weight of carrying this fear every single second of the day. 

I just want to be left alone to figure this out. I want my body to be mine again. And more than anything, I want the people who are supposed to be "caring" for me to see the girl behind the growing belly and realize that she’s scared, not stupid. Being a mother doesn't mean I’ve stopped being a person, but sometimes it feels like that’s exactly what the world expects me to be.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

Honestly, the first thing I ever did—before I even stepped foot in that clinic—was go straight to my phone. I’m sitting there in the kitchen, the sunlight hitting the table just right, and I’m terrified to tell a single soul I know. So, I turned to the only thing that didn’t require me to explain myself: the forums. 

I started typing in my symptoms, looking for stories from other girls who were young and scared and didn't have anyone in their corner. I wasn't looking for medical advice, not really. I was looking for someone to tell me it wasn't the end of the world. I was looking for a reflection of myself. 

But what I found were these sanitized spaces where everyone seems to be in a perfect marriage with a nursery already planned. Even when I tried to look for "single mom" forums, it felt like people were either giving super-tough advice or just talking about custody battles and ex-partners. Nobody was talking about the quiet, heavy terror of just being nineteen and trying to figure out if you're going to be okay.

I didn't turn to my mother, and I certainly didn't turn to my friends. I couldn't bear the look in their eyes. So, the internet became my only "community." It’s a lonely place to find comfort, honestly, but it was better than the silence of this house. It feels like I’m constantly shouting into a void, hoping someone will shout back, "Me too, Agnes, I’m here and I’m scared, too." But mostly, the void just stays quiet.

It’s the irony of it, I think. We live in a world where you can find anything on a screen—every symptom, every potential horror story, every perfect product recommendation—but you can’t find a *person*. You can’t find a hand to hold when you’re shaking in the kitchen. 

When I look at those forums, I’m searching for a mirror. I’m looking for someone who sounds like me, someone who isn’t pretending that pregnancy is this mystical, blissful "golden time." But the people who are honest about the fear and the isolation get drowned out by the noise of the "perfect" ones. It makes me feel like my experience—the struggle of being nineteen, the lack of support, the fear of the future—is a private failure rather than a common reality.

If I had someone—not a doctor with a clipboard, not a judgey neighbor, but someone who had been there—who could just say, "You’re doing a good job, and it’s okay to be terrified," I think I could breathe. I think I could actually enjoy this, even just a little bit. Instead, I’m just managing. I’m managing the doctor’s indifference, I’m managing my family’s silence, and I’m managing my own heart. I just hope that when the baby finally arrives, I’ll be able to see them as a person and not just another thing I have to "manage" to keep safe in a world that hasn't made me feel safe yet.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

I can honestly count on one hand the number of times I felt like I was actually *in* the conversation. Most of the time, the clinic feels like a place where they’ve already decided the script. They walk in, they check their charts, they tell me what’s happening next—the next scan, the next test, the next set of vitamins—and it’s all presented as a fact, not a choice. It’s "We’ll do this at eighteen weeks," not "What do you think about this?" or "Do you have any questions about why we're doing this?"

When I try to ask a question, I get that polite, hurried look, like I’m an interruption. I’ve learned that if I push too hard, the tone gets even colder. So, I just listen. I take the plan, I nod, and I accept that my role here is to be the vessel, not the participant. 

There was *one* time, though, a nurse actually stopped and asked, "How are you doing with the nausea? Is it making it hard to get to work?" It caught me so off-guard I almost cried. Just having someone recognize that I have a life—a job, a schedule, a set of challenges—outside of their clinic hours made the world of difference. But that was an exception. Most of the time, I’m just an object being steered through a process I didn’t design and don’t fully understand. It’s lonely, being treated like a passenger in your own pregnancy.

I keep thinking about that one nurse—the one who actually asked about my job. It makes me realize that it wouldn't take a whole new system or a ton of money to change how this feels. It just takes someone to pause. To stop looking at the screen, to stop flipping pages in a file, and to look at me—not as a nineteen-year-old girl who’s "low-risk," but as a person who’s carrying a mountain of worry on her shoulders.

But that moment was a flicker, and then it was gone. Back to the fluorescent lights and the cold, routine check-boxes. It’s hard not to feel like that kindness was an accident, or maybe just a momentary lapse in the "professional" barrier they’re supposed to keep up.

I wonder sometimes what it would be like if I had a voice in this, if I could say, "I'm terrified of these tests because they feel like they’re waiting for me to fail," and have a doctor actually sit with that instead of just telling me the risks are low. I want to feel like a collaborator, not a project.

I’m sitting here, and the fog is finally lifting off the trees, and it feels a little less suffocating than it did this morning. But the loneliness… that doesn't really lift. It’s still right there, sitting in the kitchen with me. I just hope that when the baby comes, the world is kinder to them than it’s been to me so far. I hope they don't have to fight to be seen. I hope they get to be heard.

You know, it’s a strange thing, being nineteen and pregnant. It’s like the world suddenly decides your life is a series of milestones to be managed, but nobody cares about the girl who has to reach them. I feel like I’m walking through a long, fluorescent-lit tunnel, and all I want is someone to walk alongside me, not behind me with a clipboard, taking notes on my progress.

I think about the future—the real, actual future—and I get this knot in my stomach. Not because I don't want the baby, but because I’m so afraid I’m going to lose *myself* in this process. Between the way the doctors treat me like an object and the way my family treats me like a disappointment, I’m scared I’ll wake up after the birth and forget what my own voice sounds like, or what my own dreams were before all of this became a "clinical event."

But even with all the fear, even with the metallic taste of the nausea and the silence of this house, there’s this tiny, stubborn part of me that keeps going. I look at that grainy ultrasound picture pinned to my fridge, and I feel… something. It’s not "joy" in the way they describe in the magazines. It’s more like a quiet, desperate, fierce kind of love. It’s a love that says, *I’m going to make sure you get a better start than I’m getting right now.*

Maybe that’s the one thing they can’t measure on their charts. My determination. I’m scared, yeah, and I’m lonely, and I’m tired of being treated like a mistake, but I’m here. And I’m going to keep showing up, even if I have to do it by myself. I just wish, just once, that the world would acknowledge that a nineteen-year-old girl can be a mother without having to surrender her dignity to a system that doesn't know her name. I’m Agnes. And I’m trying. I really am.
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

Every single one of them. It’s like there’s a seat in the room that’s supposed to be filled, and its emptiness is louder than anything the doctor says. When I sit on that crinkly paper on the exam table, I always catch myself looking at the empty plastic chair in the corner. I think about what it would be like if someone were sitting there, holding my hand, or just *knowing* what the ultrasound looks like so I didn’t have to carry that image home and stare at it in the dark by myself.

The isolation really hits when the provider asks, "So, did you bring anyone with you today?" or "Does your partner have any questions?" Every time I have to say "No," I see that little flicker of—something. Pity? Discomfort? It doesn't matter what it is; it’s a wall. It immediately makes me feel like I’m failing a test I didn't even know I was taking. 

It’s not just about the emotional support I’m missing; it’s the logistics. Who’s going to remember the doctor’s instructions when I’m exhausted? Who’s going to help me process the fear after a long drive? I feel like I’m playing a game of catch by myself, throwing the ball against a wall and waiting for it to bounce back. It’s exhausting, and it makes me feel like I’m constantly on the defensive, trying to prove I’m enough for both of us when I’m really just struggling to be enough for myself.

The hardest thing is knowing that even if I were to suddenly have a partner, the system wouldn't change. It would just make it easier for them to stop looking at *me* and start directing their questions to *him*. It’s a lose-lose. Right now, I’m "that girl who’s alone," which feels like a label of failure. If I were with someone, I’d probably just become the "woman in the room who is being spoken for." It feels like there’s no room for me, the actual person, in any of these scenarios.

I want someone to look at me—not at the seat next to me, not at the files, not at the clock—and just acknowledge that I am a person who is doing something brave. I am a nineteen-year-old in Grafton, and I am learning how to build a life from scratch. And it’s not because I’m "low-risk." It’s because I have to be. 

Every time I walk out of that clinic, I feel like I’m holding my breath until I get back to my car, where I can finally let go. I just want to be able to talk about my baby, my body, and my fears without feeling like I’m auditioning for a role I didn't ask for. I’m just Agnes. I hope that, eventually, that will be enough. I’m going to make it enough.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

Every single time. I go online because the clinic is a closed door, and I’m standing on the outside. When I’m in the exam room, I feel this intense pressure to be "low-risk"—which means *quiet*—so I never ask the things that are really eating me up inside. I don’t ask if it’s normal to feel so completely unmoored, or if it’s okay to be this terrified of the future. I don't ask what *really* happens to your body during a delivery, because I’m afraid they’ll just give me some sterile, simplified answer that makes me feel like a child.

I look for the grit, I think. I look for the real-life, messy stuff that isn't in those glossy pamphlets. I want to know what it *feels* like to be alone in the hospital, what people say to you, and how you survive the first few days when you’re doing it on your own. I want to know if other women have felt like I do—like an imposter in their own skin. 

The pamphlets tell me how many ounces to gain and what vitamins to take. But they don't tell me how to handle the silence when I’m staring at the walls of my kitchen at 3:00 AM, wondering if I’m capable of keeping another human alive when I feel like I’m barely keeping myself together. I go online looking for humanity, but all I find is more data. It’s like I’m starving for a real conversation, and the internet is just feeding me dry, nutritional facts.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

You know, thinking back on everything we've talked about, the thing that sticks with me most is how much I’ve had to hide. I feel like I’ve been wearing this mask of "everything is fine" since the day I walked into the clinic. I hide the terror, I hide the loneliness of this empty house, and I definitely hide how much I’m actually struggling with the transition to being a mother. 

I think it’s important for someone to know—for *you* to know—that a woman can be "low-risk" on a chart, but "high-need" in her heart. Being十九, being alone, and having to navigate this system without a village… that’s a heavy weight, and the way the system is set up, it doesn't just ignore that weight, it adds to it by making you feel like you’re doing something wrong just for feeling it.

If I could reach through the screen and tell the people who write the brochures, or the doctors who rush through the appointments, one thing, it would be this: stop looking at the patient as a series of hurdles to clear. Start looking at the person who is standing in front of you. Because when you treat a scared, nineteen-year-old girl like she’s just another case number, you’re not helping her—you’re just making the journey even colder. 

I’m Agnes. I’m scared, I’m alone, and I’m growing a life. That should be enough for someone to just stop and listen. Thank you for listening to me today. It’s the first time in a long time I’ve felt like my voice actually mattered in all of this.
